title: Nutrition
content: Nutrition is the science of how food and its components (nutrients) affect the health and functioning of the body. It involves macronutrients - carbohydrates, proteins, and fats - which provide energy and the building blocks for growth and repair, and micronutrients - vitamins and minerals - which support metabolic processes in smaller amounts. Good nutrition supports growth, immune function, energy levels, and long-term disease prevention, while poor nutrition can lead to deficiencies, obesity, and chronic disease. A balanced diet includes a variety of whole foods across all food groups in appropriate portions, tailored to individual needs like age, activity level, and health conditions.
keywords: Nutrition, macronutrients, micronutrients, balanced diet, food science

title: Vitamins and Minerals (Overview)
content: Vitamins and minerals are essential micronutrients the body needs in small amounts to function properly. Vitamins are organic compounds classified as fat-soluble (A, D, E, K - stored in body fat, absorbed alongside dietary fat) or water-soluble (the B-complex vitamins and vitamin C - not stored in the body, so they need regular intake). Minerals are inorganic elements, divided into macrominerals needed in larger amounts (calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, sodium, potassium) and trace minerals needed in tiny amounts (iron, zinc, iodine, selenium, copper). Together, vitamins and minerals support energy production, immune function, bone health, nerve signaling, and countless other body processes; a varied diet is usually the best way to get adequate amounts of each, though supplementation may be needed for diagnosed deficiencies.
keywords: Vitamins, minerals, micronutrients, fat soluble, water soluble, macrominerals, trace minerals

title: Immunology
content: Immunology is the branch of biology and medicine that studies the immune system - the body's defense network against infections, foreign substances, and abnormal cells. It covers innate immunity (the body's fast, general first-line defense, like skin and white blood cells) and adaptive immunity (a slower, highly specific defense that 'remembers' past infections, involving T-cells and B-cells). Immunology also studies how the immune system can malfunction, causing allergies, autoimmune diseases, or immunodeficiency (like in HIV/AIDS). Nutrition plays a significant supporting role in immunology, since many vitamins and minerals (vitamin C, vitamin D, zinc, and others) are directly required for proper immune cell development and function.
keywords: Immunology, immune system, innate immunity, adaptive immunity, T-cells, B-cells
